[Clinician asked: "okay and do you have like a regular exercise regimen at all"] try to not consistent with it

[Clinician asked: "alright so the lateral aspect not a problem and you said that you heard a pop when you landed right"] yeah
[Clinician asked: "okay have you taken anything for the pain"] just tylenol

[Clinician asked: "okay that's fine and how long ago did this happen"] a week ago
[Clinician asked: "okay alright so you put up with the pain for a week that's i'm proud of you for that"] i do n't like to come to the doctors

[Clinician asked: "excuse me i feel rejected right now why why would you say something like that it's fine but you know i i like it when you're not here either because that means that you're doing a good job so let's see about like getting your knee fixed up but i want to talk about some of the other issues that you h"] what no
[Clinician asked: "no okay that's fine have you measured your blood pressure recently at all"] no
[Clinician asked: "okay are you still taking your medication because i see you're on twenty milligrams of lisinopril"] yeah most days i remember

[Clinician asked: "okay"] ra i'm not suspicious but we're just gon na go with that okay because looking at your vitals it it is of still a bit high i'm not really comfortable i see like a hundred and eighty over you know eighty and

[Clinician asked: "that's not where we wan na be so i wonder if we might need to adjust your medication but let's talk about your diabetes how are your blood sugars"] i think they're little bit better
[Clinician asked: "okay what makes you think that have you been taking them"] yeah i check it you know most days again kinda when i remember
[Clinician asked: "okay how often are you checking it"] i would say four five times a week
[Clinician asked: "okay you might wan na switch to maybe two or three times a day but you know that's something i'm glad that you are measuring are you taking your metformin"] yes
[Clinician asked: "okay and then i think we have you on five hundred milligrams correct"] yes
[Clinician asked: "alright how is your diet"] kinda the same as my exercise decent could be better

[Clinician asked: "alright and okay not a problem so what is your do you are you measuring your calories at all looking at your macros are you just eating what you feel like"] yeah just trying to watch what i eat more than anything but nothing specific

[Clinician asked: "okay and are you do you know if you're particularly focused on your salt like is it like a low salt diet"] no just trying to watch my carbs a little bit more but not counting or anything
[Clinician asked: "alright so like is it a specific diet i just wan na make sure like are you on like the keto diet"] no

[Clinician asked: "okay alright not a problem so if you do n't mind i'm gon na go ahead and do a quick physical exam as i mentioned before your blood pressure is a little high as i listen to your heart i do like it's got a nice regular rate and rhythm i do n't appreciate any murmur when i listen to your lungs they sou"] little bit
[Clinician asked: "okay and then when i press on the inside does it hurt"] no
[Clinician asked: "alright can you bend your knee and straighten it"] i can i think that's all i can i can bend
[Clinician asked: "mm-hmm alright so are you having problems walking can you bear weight"] i can but i have a little bit of a limp

[Clinician asked: "okay so do you mind getting up and walking for me really quickly alright so i do notice that there is a slight gait like there is a small sorry you are correcting you do have a limp i i am a little worried about that but it's probably it's probably the superficial when i'm looking at your knee i do "] sounds good
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Right knee injury.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

William Russell is a pleasant 57-year-old male who presents to the clinic today for the evaluation of a right knee injury. He has a past medical history significant for hypertension and type 2 diabetes.

Approximately 1 week ago, the patient sustained an injury to his right knee while at a trampoline park. He explains that he was jumping on a trampoline when he heard a snap, followed by instant pain in the lateral aspect of his knee. This is not a common activity for him, and he admits that he is inconsistent with exercise. As far as treatment, he has been elevating his knee and taking Tylenol without significant relief of his pain. He has not tried applying heat or ice to the area.

In terms of his hypertension, the patient admits that he did not purchase a blood pressure cuff as previously recommended. While he does not monitor his blood pressures at home, he has been compliant with lisinopril 20 mg.

His diabetes appears to be stable based on home monitoring, although he is only checking his blood glucose levels 4 to 5 times per week. He does not follow any specific diet plan, but he does try to make healthier choices such as limiting his carbohydrate intake. He has also been taking his metformin 500 mg as prescribed.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Musculoskeletal: Reports right knee pain.

VITALS

Blood pressure: 180/80 mmHg

PHYSICAL EXAM

CV: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs.
RESPIRATORY:
Lungs are clear bilaterally.
MSK: Examination of the right knee: Mild tenderness to palpation at the lateral aspect. Ecchymosis and edema along the lateral aspect. No effusion. Decent range of motion. Pain with movement.

RESULTS

X-rays of the right knee were obtained and reviewed. These reveal no evidence of fracture or bony abnormality.

ASSESSMENT

1. Right knee pain, lateral ligament strain.
2. Hypertension.
3. Diabetes type 2.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination and radiographic findings today, his symptoms appear to be consistent with a lateral ligament strain of the right knee. I am going to prescribe meloxicam 15 mg once daily for pain and swelling. I also put in a referral for physical therapy so he can work on strengthening the muscles around the area and prevent any further injury. We can consider further imaging if he continues to have pain, but I explained that this is a common injury that typically heals on it's own.

For his hypertension, I'm concerned that his blood pressure is not well controlled at this time, so we may need to modify his medication. I'm going to order an echocardiogram for further evaluation, but I want him to continue taking his lisinopril 20 mg for now.

For his diabetes, he stated that he has been measuring his blood glucose levels 4 to 5 times per week, but I want him to start measuring them 2 to 3 times per day. His hemoglobin A1c levels should provide more detail as far as his long-term progress so we will order that today. I'm also going to order a lipid panel and we can determine if any adjustments need to be made to his medication. In the meantime, he should continue taking metformin 500 mg.

naive.response_text: I understand how concerning your knee injury must be, especially after hearing the pop and experiencing that sudden pain. It’s important to take this seriously, and I’m glad you came in to get it checked out. Based on your symptoms and the history you provided, it sounds like we might need to look into whether there’s a ligament or meniscus injury. I’m going to recommend an MRI to get a clearer picture of what’s going on. In the meantime, try to rest and avoid activities that make the pain worse. We’ll work together to figure this out and get you back on your feet. Also, let’s keep an eye on your blood pressure and diabetes management. We’ll adjust medications if needed and encourage you to continue monitoring your health. Please feel free to ask any questions or share any concerns you have—I’m here to help.

strong_prompt.response_text:
William, thank you for sharing all this with me. It sounds like this knee injury is really bothering you, and it's completely understandable to feel worried after hearing a pop and experiencing persistent pain, especially when it's something new like a trampoline park. I can tell you're trying to manage it on your own, which is commendable, but I'm glad you're here so we can figure this out together.

Given that this is your first time doing something like this and it's been a week with little improvement, I want to make sure we get to the bottom of it. I've done a quick exam and noticed a slight limp, which could be due to the injury. To better understand what's going on, we might need to look into imaging, like an MRI or X-ray, to check for any tears or sprains. I'll explain everything in plain terms, and we'll decide together on the next steps.

We'll start with the physical exam and possibly schedule an imaging test if needed. In the meantime, I'll show you some exercises to help with the pain and swelling. Before you leave, can you summarize what we discussed so I can ensure I've covered everything clearly? We'll follow up in a week or two to see how you're doing.

framework_only.response_text:
Thank you for sharing your experience with the knee injury. It sounds like you're dealing with some discomfort and challenges, and I'm here to help address those.

I want to make sure I explained this clearly — can you tell me, in your own words, what we're going to do next and why?

Let's plan to see each other again in a few days. In the meantime, if your symptoms get worse or you experience severe pain, please call us right away.
